narrative:
Steny Hamilton Hoyer has been in Congress since 1981 — long enough to have voted on issues that are now in history textbooks, and long enough that the term "career politician" feels less like an insult and more like an understatement. First elected in a special election when Ronald Reagan was still settling into the Oval Office, Hoyer went on to serve as the second-most-powerful Democrat in the House for two decades, holding the title of Majority Leader under Nancy Pelosi from 2007 to 2011 and again from 2019 to 2023 [1]. In January 2026, at age 86, he announced his retirement — ending a six-decade career in elected office that began in the Maryland State Senate in 1966 [2]. If there were a term limits bill in 1981, Hoyer would have been out before the internet was invented.

Hoyer earned an "A" rating from the End Citizens United/Let America Vote Action Fund for his work on campaign finance reform, and he has championed the DISCLOSE Act and the For the People Act as legislative priorities [3]. He has spoken passionately about getting dark money out of politics. And yet, as House Majority Leader, Hoyer was given a fundraising target of $900,000 by the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee — a figure that is difficult to hit without corporate PAC money [4]. Just 14.1 percent of his direct campaign contributions came from small donors, while corporate PACs provided the bulk of his war chest [4]. He has talked the talk on reform while walking a well-worn path to the same fundraising dinners he publicly decries.

The tension between Hoyer's rhetoric and his donor list was on vivid display during the 2021 eviction moratorium crisis. As the federal moratorium was set to expire — threatening hundreds of thousands of families with displacement — Hoyer attempted to extend it by unanimous consent, a procedural maneuver almost guaranteed to fail when a single Republican objection could kill it [5]. What made the tepid response particularly notable was Hoyer's donor sheet: in the 2020 cycle, he had accepted $5,000 from Vanguard's PAC, $5,000 from Goldman Sachs, $2,000 from the Carlyle Group, and $2,500 from Invesco — Wall Street firms that had been aggressively buying up single-family homes [6]. When asked about the crisis, Hoyer expressed concern for "the welfare of both renters and landlords," a carefully balanced sentiment that weighed the anxieties of million-dollar investment funds against families facing eviction.

On many of the 80% Bill's core priorities, Hoyer's record is genuinely strong. He has co-sponsored paid family leave legislation, voted for police reform and body camera mandates, supported Medicare drug price negotiation, and backed voting rights protections throughout his career [7]. He submitted testimony advocating for twelve weeks of paid leave for federal employees and supported the broader FAMILY Act. His lifetime voting record on civil rights earned a perfect 100% from the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights [8]. These are not trivial accomplishments, and they reflect a legislator who has used his considerable power to advance popular causes.

But Hoyer's career also illustrates the central paradox the 80% Bill is designed to expose: a congressman can support nearly every popular reform on paper while being sustained by the very system those reforms would dismantle. After more than four decades in office, Hoyer leaves behind a legacy of genuine legislative achievement — and a fundraising apparatus that demonstrates exactly why Congress remains so resistant to change. He is the rare politician who both proves the case for reform and embodies the reason it never quite arrives.

Mazie Hirono's origin story is the kind that would strain credulity in fiction. Born in Fukushima, Japan, she was raised on her grandparents' rice farm until her mother -- fleeing an alcoholic, gambling-addicted husband -- literally escaped to Hawaii with young Mazie in tow [1]. The girl who arrived in Honolulu at age seven speaking no English would grow up to become the first Asian-American woman elected to the U.S. Senate, the first senator born in Japan, and one of the most reliably progressive voices in Congress [2]. She now sits on the Judiciary, Armed Services, Energy and Natural Resources, Veterans Affairs, and Small Business committees -- a portfolio that touches nearly every plank of the 80% Bill [3].

On the substance of those twenty proposals, Hirono's record is unusually strong. She is a cosponsor of the ETHICS Act, comprehensive legislation to ban congressional stock trading by members, their spouses, and dependent children, stating that "Americans should have confidence that Members of Congress are acting in the public's best interest" [4]. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act and its landmark Medicare drug price negotiation provisions [5]. She has introduced bicameral legislation to strengthen and expand Social Security by phasing out the payroll tax cap so the wealthiest Americans pay their fair share, extending the program's solvency to 2052 [6]. She cosponsored the Carried Interest Fairness Act to close the tax loophole that lets hedge fund managers pay lower rates than their secretaries [7]. And she joined the American Homeownership Act to curb Wall Street's bulk purchasing of single-family homes [8].

On campaign finance and transparency, Hirono has been a cosponsor of legislation to force corporate disclosure of political spending and joined colleagues in demanding transparency from dark money groups [9]. She has voted with Bernie Sanders on resolutions to apply human rights conditions to military aid -- not exactly a risk-free position in a state with the nation's highest per-capita military presence [10]. Her Armed Services Committee seat, where she serves as Ranking Member of the Readiness and Management Support Subcommittee, gives her real leverage over Pentagon accountability, though she has stopped short of championing a full Pentagon audit [11].

The money side of Hirono's ledger is comparatively clean but not immaculate. Lawyers and law firms have been her top contributing industry throughout her career, and she has accepted contributions from defense-related PACs -- unsurprising for a senator on Armed Services whose state hosts Pearl Harbor, Schofield Barracks, and a constellation of Pacific Command installations [12]. But she has not approached anything like her House colleague Ed Case's near-total PAC dependency, and her willingness to cosponsor the ETHICS Act and carried interest reform suggests she is at least willing to bite the hands that occasionally feed her.

Hirono is not a perfect match for all twenty planks -- she has not championed term limits, and her record on a full AUMF repeal or Pentagon audit remains incomplete. But for a senator who escaped poverty in postwar Japan, she has shown a consistent willingness to side with the popular supermajority positions that define the 80% Bill, even when doing so puts her crosswise with powerful industries in her own state. In Congress, that counts as something close to courage.

Martin Heinrich grew up in Cole Camp, Missouri, the son of a telephone lineman and a factory worker, before heading to the University of Missouri for a mechanical engineering degree [1]. He moved to Albuquerque to work on directed-energy weapons research at what is now the Air Force Research Laboratory on Kirtland Air Force Base [2] — an early preview of a career spent straddling the line between clean-energy idealist and defense-dollar pragmatist.

Today Heinrich sits as Ranking Member of the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee and serves on the Appropriations Committee, where he has steered billions toward Sandia and Los Alamos National Laboratories [3]. In the FY2025 NDAA alone, he secured over $277 million for environmental cleanup at the two labs and authorized $1.79 billion for LANL research programs in FY2026 [4]. His top donor industries for 2023-2024 tell the story neatly: Electric Utilities ranked first, Renewable Energy second, and Indian Gaming third [5]. PAC money accounted for roughly 22 percent of his 2019-2024 fundraising haul, with large individual contributions making up about 60 percent [5].

On the 80% Bill's marquee items, Heinrich checks most of the boxes. He is an original cosponsor of the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act alongside Ossoff and Lujan [6]. He voted to repeal the 2002 Iraq AUMF when the Senate passed S. 316 by a 66-30 margin [7]. He cosponsored the FAMILY Act for 12 weeks of paid leave [8] and has repeatedly cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to force donor transparency above $10,000 [9]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug-price negotiation provisions, and he authored electrification measures within it [10]. He introduced the Freedom to Vote Act with Lujan to end partisan gerrymandering [11].

Where Heinrich gets complicated is the defense portfolio. He championed expanded production of plutonium pit bomb cores — the triggers inside nuclear warheads — at a time when he was simultaneously branding himself as a climate hawk who co-founded the Senate Electrification Caucus [3][10]. His leadership PAC, the aptly named "Lobo PAC," funnels resources to fellow Democrats while his office celebrates routing $142 million in congressionally directed spending to 134 New Mexico projects in a single fiscal year [12]. That is not corruption; it is constituent service on steroids. But it is also a reminder that even the most reform-minded senators have incentive structures that the 80% Bill is designed to disrupt.

Heinrich has not cosponsored a congressional term-limits amendment — which, given that he is now serving a third Senate term stretching to 2031, is perhaps unsurprising [2]. He has, however, been willing to cosponsor legislation that would cost him personally, including the stock-trading ban. Whether that willingness extends to every plank of the 80% Bill remains to be seen.

Jim Himes has represented Connecticut's 4th district since 2009, bringing to Congress a resume that reads like a Wall Street starter pack: twelve years at Goldman Sachs, a home in Greenwich among the hedge fund elite, and a seat on the House Financial Services Committee that lets him keep one wingtip firmly planted in the world he supposedly left behind [1][2]. He is also the ranking member of the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, making him a member of the elite "Gang of Eight" privy to the nation's most sensitive secrets [3]. His estimated net worth of roughly $9.3 million places him comfortably among the wealthiest members of Congress [4].

On paper, Himes checks a surprising number of 80% Bill boxes. He helped pass the STOCK Act banning congressional insider trading and later introduced the Insider Trading Prohibition Act to strengthen federal law [5][6]. He has repeatedly introduced the Reclamation of War Powers Act to claw back Congress's constitutional authority to declare war, and he voted to repeal the 2002 Iraq AUMF [7][8]. He co-sponsored a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United, backed the DISCLOSE Act, and supported the Close the Floodgates Act to reinstate individual contribution limits [9]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which empowered Medicare to negotiate drug prices for the first time, calling it "a historic win for the American people" [10]. On election reform, he has supported anti-gerrymandering measures and campaign finance transparency legislation.

But the contradictions pile up like derivatives on a balance sheet. Himes introduced legislation to gut a key Dodd-Frank provision requiring banks to move risky derivatives trading out of taxpayer-insured units — a bill that read as if Citigroup's lobbyists had drafted it, which, given that Citigroup was his number-one career donor, was not an unreasonable suspicion [11][12]. He was one of sixteen Democrats to vote against an antitrust package cracking down on anti-competitive corporate behavior [13]. He chairs the New Democrat Coalition, the caucus most sympathetic to Wall Street's regulatory wish list. The man who champions banning congressional stock trading still reports personal stock transactions through STOCK Act filings — technically legal, but a reminder that the reforms he advocates have yet to actually constrain his own portfolio [14].

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Only four percent of Himes's fundraising comes from small donors; thirty-four percent flows from PACs, and sixty percent from large individual contributors [1]. Six of his top ten career donors are mega-banks [2]. The securities and investment industry has been his financial backbone throughout his career, and he has repaid that loyalty with votes to weaken the very regulations designed to prevent another 2008 meltdown [11][12]. He represents Greenwich, Connecticut — America's hedge fund capital — and his legislative record on financial regulation tracks suspiciously well with his zip code's interests.

The bottom line: Jim Himes is a Democrat who talks reform while walking a tightrope above Wall Street's safety net. He deserves real credit for his war powers work and campaign finance rhetoric, but when the Financial Services Committee gavel comes down on banking regulation, his Goldman Sachs muscle memory kicks in. His constituents in Fairfield County get a congressman who sounds like Elizabeth Warren at town halls and votes like a managing director at committee markups.

Andy Harris is a man who understands the value of a promise — right up until it becomes inconvenient. The Johns Hopkins-trained anesthesiologist rode into Congress in 2010 on the Tea Party wave, pledging to Eastern Shore voters that he would serve no more than six terms. Twelve years, he said, was plenty of time to fix Washington. Then 2022 rolled around, and Harris decided that actually, Washington still needed fixing — by him, specifically — and filed for a seventh term [1]. It turns out the only term limit Andy Harris supports is the kind that applies to other people.

As chairman of the House Freedom Caucus and a senior member of the Appropriations Committee — where he chairs the Agriculture, Rural Development, and FDA subcommittee — Harris wields significant power over federal spending on food safety, drug regulation, and farm policy [2]. He has received substantial campaign contributions from the health products and pharmaceutical industries over his career, which makes his committee oversight of the FDA a convenient arrangement for everyone involved except, perhaps, the patients [3]. Harris voted against the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, siding with every other House Republican against letting the government bargain down the cost of prescription drugs that his own constituents struggle to afford [4].

Harris co-sponsored a congressional stock trading ban in the 119th Congress, a welcome if belated conversion to the cause of financial transparency [5]. But his record on other 80% Bill priorities tells a different story. He has shown no interest in auditing the Pentagon, banning corporate PACs, or passing the DISCLOSE Act — the kind of good-government reforms that might make his Freedom Caucus colleagues uncomfortable at fundraisers. He has opposed expanding paid family leave, and his record on voting rights and fair elections is shaped more by partisan advantage than democratic principle.

The congressman is perhaps best known nationally for his years-long crusade to block Washington, D.C. from legalizing marijuana sales — the infamous "Harris Rider" that overrode the will of D.C. voters who approved legalization by a 70-30 margin [6]. For a self-described champion of limited government, Harris has shown a remarkable enthusiasm for telling other people's constituents how to govern themselves. He was also present at a December 21, 2020 White House meeting with Republican House members to discuss plans to challenge the 2020 election results on January 6th, and days after the Capitol breach, set off the newly installed metal detectors while trying to bring a firearm onto the House floor [7].

Harris represents Maryland's 1st District, a largely rural Eastern Shore seat he has openly acknowledged was drawn to elect a Republican [8]. He has called for independent redistricting commissions — a position that aligns with the 80% Bill's Fair Elections article — though his enthusiasm for that reform has not translated into action on most of the other consensus priorities that his own constituents overwhelmingly support. The doctor from Baltimore who promised to be a citizen-legislator has become, after fifteen years in office, exactly the kind of career politician he once campaigned against.

Bill Huizenga is a Republican who ran a gravel company, worked in real estate, and then discovered that the real money in Michigan politics comes from knowing the right people in West Michigan's donor class. A Calvin College graduate and former aide to Rep. Pete Hoekstra, Huizenga won his House seat in 2010 and has held it ever since, currently representing Michigan's 4th district [1]. His top career contributor is Amway — the multi-level marketing empire owned by the DeVos family — which has funneled over $128,000 to his campaigns through its various entities, making the billionaire clan his single largest patron [2]. He is also a former classmate of Erik Prince, the Blackwater USA founder and Betsy DeVos's brother, which gives his corner of the Grand Rapids establishment a remarkably small-world quality.

Huizenga's professional home in Congress is the House Financial Services Committee, where he serves as vice chair and heads the Oversight and Investigations Subcommittee [3]. His top contributing industry over his career is Securities and Investment, which has sent him over $1.35 million [4]. In return, Huizenga has been a reliable voice for deregulation, criticizing the SEC under former Chair Gary Gensler for allegedly "stifling innovation" and championing crypto-friendly legislation like the FIT21 Act and the CLARITY Act [5]. He has called cryptocurrency "the future" — a conviction that happens to align neatly with the financial interests of his donor base.

Perhaps the most revealing Huizenga moment came in 2017, when he told MLive.com that he had delayed taking his son to the emergency room after the boy broke his arm, opting to wait until the next day to see a regular doctor, citing the "cost difference" [6]. He offered this as an example of the kind of consumer-driven healthcare decision-making he wanted to encourage through Republican health reform. The backlash was immediate and bipartisan. Huizenga later walked the story back, telling the Associated Press that the broken arm was "a bad example" and that cost had not actually driven his decision — his family has Blue Cross Blue Shield coverage [6]. The episode neatly captured the gap between the free-market healthcare talking points Huizenga sells on Capitol Hill and the reality that he, a sitting congressman with employer-provided insurance, was never actually forced to make that choice.

On the 80% Bill's core issues, Huizenga is a near-total miss. He opposes banning congressional stock trading, has voted against campaign finance reform, and has shown no interest in auditing the Pentagon or expanding Medicare drug negotiation [1]. He voted against the Respect for Marriage Act and describes himself as an opponent of the Affordable Care Act [1]. His district in southwestern Michigan — Kalamazoo, Battle Creek, Holland — keeps sending him back, most recently with a comfortable win over Democrat Jessica Swartz in 2024 [1].

John Hoeven is the senior senator from North Dakota and one of the wealthiest members of the United States Senate, with a net worth estimated between $22 million and $97 million depending on how you read his financial disclosures [1]. Before entering politics, Hoeven was a banker — most notably president of the Bank of North Dakota, the nation's only state-owned bank — and at one point owned 39% of Westbrand, Inc., the parent company of First Western Bank & Trust [2]. He served as governor from 2000 to 2010 before ascending to the Senate, where he has been a tireless champion of oil pipelines and a quiet opponent of nearly every plank in the 80% Bill.

Hoeven was one of the Senate's leading advocates for the Keystone XL Pipeline, organizing bipartisan letters and introducing legislation to force its approval [3]. He has consistently pushed to expand oil and gas drilling in the Bakken formation and fought the Biden administration's natural gas regulations tooth and nail [4]. Like every Senate Republican, he voted against the Inflation Reduction Act and its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, arguing that price controls would 'just shift the expense from Medicare to private pay' [5]. He voted against the Freedom to Vote Act, which would have banned partisan gerrymandering and restored the Voting Rights Act [6].

Then there is the stock trading. In January 2020, five days after attending a classified Senate briefing on the emerging coronavirus pandemic, Hoeven purchased up to $250,000 in a health sciences fund [7]. His office said the purchase was approved weeks beforehand and coincided with the fund's IPO — an explanation that satisfied investigators but left a sour taste given that millions of Americans were about to lose their savings while senators were quietly repositioning theirs. Despite this episode, Hoeven has not co-sponsored the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act.

On the AFL-CIO's legislative scorecard, Hoeven consistently scores near zero, opposing labor rights, wages, consumer protection, and financial regulation [8]. He has taken no position on banning corporate PACs, the DISCLOSE Act, closing the carried interest loophole, paid family leave, term limits, or a lifetime lobbying ban. North Dakota's senior senator is worth tens of millions, sits on committees that shape energy and agriculture policy, and has never met a pipeline he did not like or a transparency bill he did [9].

Steven Horsford's biography reads like a Democratic campaign ad written by someone who genuinely lived it. Raised by a single mother in Las Vegas, he worked multiple jobs, became the first in his family to attend college, and went on to lead the Culinary Training Academy -- Nevada's largest job-training program -- for more than a decade [1]. He was the first African American to serve as Nevada Senate Majority Leader and the first to represent the state in Congress [2]. He chaired the Congressional Black Caucus from 2023 to 2025 [3]. The resume is real.

So is the donor sheet. In the 2023-2024 cycle, Horsford's top organizational contributor was the American Israel Public Affairs Committee (AIPAC) at $117,875, and his top industry was Securities & Investment at $575,869 [4]. Large individual contributions made up 47.77% of his funding, PAC money accounted for another 47.40%, and small-dollar donations -- the kind that signal grassroots enthusiasm -- clocked in at a microscopic 3.38% [4]. His leadership PAC, 'Blue Nevada PAC,' further entangles him in the transactional machinery of Washington fundraising [5].

On housing, Horsford talks a strong game. He introduced the HOME Act to crack down on institutional investors who manipulate housing prices and co-authored letters to the Trump administration demanding accountability for corporate landlords [6]. He launched a housing-affordability platform after an 'extensive listening campaign' across NV-4 [7]. But his bill focuses on transparency and monitoring rather than the excise-tax penalty approach in the 80% Bill that would actually make corporate home-buying unprofitable.

On paid leave, he has been a genuine champion -- pressuring House leadership to restore paid family leave to the Build Back Better Act and voting to advance universal paid leave through the Ways and Means Committee [8][9]. He has also pushed for veterans' funding and military family support [10].

The contradiction at the center of Horsford's career is the gap between his working-class origin story and his Wall Street donor base. You cannot take nearly $576,000 from the securities industry and then credibly champion closing the carried-interest loophole -- and indeed, Horsford has appeared alongside the American Investment Council praising private equity's 'critical investments' [11]. From Jan 2013 to March 2026, he missed 226 of 4,846 roll-call votes -- a 4.7% absence rate, well above the 2.1% median for current representatives [12]. When you are funded by the people you should be regulating, showing up to vote becomes optional.

Richard Hudson spent a decade as a congressional staffer before becoming a congressman himself, which is a bit like a casino dealer deciding to sit down at the table. He served as chief of staff to Reps. Virginia Foxx, John Carter, and Mike Conaway before launching his own Cabarrus Marketing Group consulting firm in 2011 -- which he conveniently dissolved the moment he won his own seat in 2012 [1]. He has represented North Carolina's 9th District ever since, rising to become the fifth-ranking Republican in House leadership as chairman of the National Republican Congressional Committee, the party's campaign fundraising arm [2].

This is a man whose job is literally to raise money for Republican candidates, and his donor list reads accordingly. In 2020, Hudson was the top pharmaceutical PAC recipient in all of Congress, pocketing $240,600 from the industry whose drug prices he is supposed to regulate as a member of the Energy and Commerce Committee's Health Subcommittee [3][4]. He sits on the very committee that writes America's healthcare laws while cashing checks from the companies those laws govern. He has voted to repeal the Affordable Care Act, declaring "I made a promise to my constituents" [5]. The promise, apparently, was to take their healthcare away.

Hudson's signature legislative achievement is the Concealed Carry Reciprocity Act, which he has introduced in five consecutive Congresses. The bill would force every state to honor concealed carry permits from every other state, regardless of local gun laws [6]. He carries an A+ rating from the NRA and a 100% score from the National Right to Life Committee [7]. His voting attendance record -- 345 of 7,381 roll call votes missed (4.7%) over his career -- is "much worse" than the median, according to GovTrack, though he has been busier raising money for the NRCC than casting votes [8].

Hudson voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which cut over $1 trillion from health programs and $120 billion from SNAP [9]. He chairs the Communications and Technology Subcommittee, where he champions rural broadband expansion -- one of the few positions that might actually help his constituents, assuming they still have health insurance to pay the internet bill [10].

On the 80% Bill's platform, Hudson is a comprehensive obstacle. He opposes Medicare drug negotiation while accepting pharma money, opposes dark money disclosure while running the GOP's dark money machine, and has shown no interest in stock trading bans, Pentagon audits, or tax loophole reforms. He is the system working exactly as designed -- for everyone except the people who vote for him.

Jared Huffman has represented California's 2nd Congressional District since 2013, a sprawling stretch of Northern California coastline that runs from the Golden Gate Bridge to the Oregon border. A former environmental lawyer and state legislator, Huffman is an avowed humanist — one of the few openly nonreligious members of Congress — who co-founded the Congressional Freethought Caucus [1]. He sits on the Natural Resources Committee, where his environmental bona fides are well established. On paper, Huffman looks like a near-perfect match for the 80% Bill. The question is whether "near-perfect" is good enough.

On the populist consensus items, Huffman checks most boxes with genuine enthusiasm. He co-introduced the Democracy for All Amendment to overturn Citizens United, calling the ruling a force that has "corroded our system of democracy" by letting "special interests spend unlimited amounts of secret money" [2]. He co-sponsored the Justice in Policing Act of 2020, which mandates police body cameras for federal officers and pushes state and local departments to follow suit [3]. He has championed Medicare drug negotiation, co-sponsored the Social Security 2100 Act to strengthen benefits rather than cut them [4], and vocally supported paid family leave, arguing that workers should never have to "choose between their paycheck and their health" [5]. On war powers, Huffman pushed to repeal the outdated AUMFs that have enabled executive war-making for two decades, and backed an amendment to slash $100 billion from the Pentagon budget [6][7].

Where Huffman earns extra credit — and where many Democrats merely offer lip service — is his willingness to take on defense spending directly. He voted to advance NDAA reform while explicitly conditioning his final vote on whether the reforms survived Senate negotiation, a rare show of legislative backbone [7]. His 100% voting alignment with President Biden in the 117th Congress suggests reliable partisanship rather than maverick independence, but on the 80% Bill's core issues, that partisanship happens to align with supermajority public opinion [8].

Follow the money, and Huffman's story stays relatively clean — if unsurprising. His campaign financing draws heavily from labor unions, environmental groups, and small donors, the kind of coalition you would expect from a deep-blue Northern California district [9]. He does accept PAC contributions from organized labor, which may give pause to purists demanding a complete ban on corporate and organizational PACs. But Huffman is not swimming in defense contractor cash or pharmaceutical money. His OpenSecrets profile shows a lawmaker whose donor base largely mirrors his voting record — a rarity in Congress, and one that makes accusations of hypocrisy harder to sustain [10]. He has endorsed banning congressional stock trading, and unlike certain colleagues, does not appear to have a side hustle trading on insider knowledge.

The bottom line: Huffman is one of the easier sells for the 80% Bill. He has co-sponsored or publicly backed at least 14 of its 20 provisions, and there is no glaring betrayal lurking in his donor records. His weakness, if you can call it one, is that he represents a district so safely Democratic that his progressive votes cost him nothing politically. The real test of conviction comes when the vote is hard — and in CA-2, it rarely is.
